coredata.dc:title: Facile and large-scale synthesis of graphene quantum dots for selective targeting and imaging of cell nucleus and mitochondria
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Zengjie
Nie, Yingying
Wei, Yuan
Zhao, Jiayuan
Liao, Xiaozhu
Zhang, Jingxiang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have good biocompatibility, high luminescence, and low photobleaching properties, which make them promising alternatives to fluorescent dyes for cell imaging. However, most of the reported GQDs lack targeted selectivity that limits their applications in biomedicine. To overcome the drawback, novel GQDs modified with polyethyleneimine (PEI) or (3-carboxyl) phenyl bromide phosphine (TPP) were originally synthesized via a facile, low-cost, environmentally friendly, and large-scale preparation method. The GQDs-PEI was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process, and then TPP was conjugated to the GQDs-PEI via the amide linkage. The physicochemical, optical, biocompatible, and targeted imaging properties were evaluated systematically. The results indicated that the average sizes of the as-produced GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were 3.75 and 3.25 nm, respectively. More significantly, the two composites had excellent optical property, low cytotoxicity and selective targeting and imaging properties for cell nucleus or mitochondria, suggesting their promising applications as the cell nucleus imaging or mitochondria imaging in vivo and in vitro for diagnosis and therapy of some related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cell nucleus targeting
Mitochondria targeting
Bioimaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

Medical Subject Fund of Stomatology College of Lanzhou University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We deeply appreciate the supports from Natural Science Foundation of China ( 81571829 ), the Medical Subject Fund of Stomatology College of Lanzhou University ( 201502-3 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( lzujbky-2017-145 , lzujbky-2017-it47 ), the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences ( LSL-1505 ), Cooperation Project between Gansu Academy of Sciences and Chinese Academy of Sciences ( 2016HZ-03 ), and Science and Technology Plan of Gansu Province ( 18JR3RA249 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP.
FTIR spectra of PEI, GQDs-PEI, TPP, and GQDs-TPP.

XPS survey spectra of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP(A), high-resolution C1s spectra of GQDs-PEI (B) and GQDs-TPP (C), high-resolution N1s spectra of GQDs-PEI (D)and GQDs-TPP (E) and high-resolution P2p spectra of GQDs-TPP (F).

TEM images of GQDs-PEI (A) and GQDs-TPP (B), the insets are the diagrams of the size distribution of both samples. AFM images of GQDs-PEI (C) and GQDs-TPP (D) with scale 5 × 5 μm, the insets are height profiles along the lines.

The optical properties of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP, (A) and (C) The UV–vis absorption spectrum, PL, and excitation (PLE) spectra, and the insets are the photographs taken under UV–vis radiation. (B) and (D) PL spectra excited at different wavelengths.
Cellular viability assay, PEI, TPP, GQDs-PEI, and GQDs-TPP with different concentrations co-cultured with Hela cells for 24 h.

The cell imaging, DAPI (A), GQDs-PEI (B), and Mito-Tracker (C) co-cultured with Hela cells for 4 h (D). The image of nucleus co-localization between DAPI and GQDs-PEI obtained by the software Image J (E). The cell imaging, DAPI (F), GQDs-TPP (G), and Mito-Tracker (H) co-cultured with Hela cells for 4 h (I). The image of nucleus co-localization between DAPI and GQDs-PEI obtained by the software Imag J (J).

head.ce:title: Facile and large-scale synthesis of graphene quantum dots for selective targeting and imaging of cell nucleus and mitochondria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Stomatology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Stomatology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Sensing Technology, Gansu Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Sensing Technology, Gansu Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have good biocompatibility, high luminescence, and low photobleaching properties, which make them promising alternatives to fluorescent dyes for cell imaging. However, most of the reported GQDs lack targeted selectivity that limits their applications in biomedicine. To overcome the drawback, novel GQDs modified with polyethyleneimine (PEI) or (3-carboxyl) phenyl bromide phosphine (TPP) were originally synthesized via a facile, low-cost, environmentally friendly, and large-scale preparation method. The GQDs-PEI was synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process, and then TPP was conjugated to the GQDs-PEI via the amide linkage. The physicochemical, optical, biocompatible, and targeted imaging properties were evaluated systematically. The results indicated that the average sizes of the as-produced GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were 3.75 and 3.25 nm, respectively. More significantly, the two composites had excellent optical property, low cytotoxicity and selective targeting and imaging properties for cell nucleus or mitochondria, suggesting their promising applications as the cell nucleus imaging or mitochondria imaging in vivo and in vitro for diagnosis and therapy of some related diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have high luminescence and upconversion luminescence [1], good biocompatibility [2], well dispersibility in water [3], and low photobleaching [4]. These excellent properties of GQDs make them superior to the traditional fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [5], which have gained increasing attention in the fields of bioimaging [6–8], optical sensors [9,10], photovoltaic devices [11,12] and light emitting diodes [13,14], et al. In particular, GQDs can be used as luminescent probes or contrast agents to visualize cells or tissues in the bioimaging. Most studies showed that GQDs can easily enter and accumulate in the cytoplasm or the cytomembrane by means of endocytosis, so the cellular contour can be revealed under special excitation wavelength. For example, Liu et al. prepared nitrogen-doped GQDs by a facile solvothermal method and found the cytoplasm could be labeled by the GQDs [6]. Peng et al. broke down the carbon fibers to GQDs by chemical-exfoliation and confirmed that the synthesized GQDs could be used to image the cell by labeling the cytoplasm [15]. Zhang et al. synthesized GQDs by electrochemical method and demonstrated that the GQDs could be used for labeling stem cell by the cytoplasm imaging [16]. Although various methods have been attempted to synthesize GQDs, the synthesized GQDs still lacked targeted selectivity, resulting in random entry and accumulation of GQDs in the cells, which limited their applications in imaging some special organelles, such as the cell nucleus or mitochondria.

As is known to all, the cell nucleus and mitochondria are two important organelles. The cell nucleus is the center of heredity and metabolization because it contains most of the genetic material of the cells, DNA. The changes of the organizational architecture of the cell nucleus are closely associated with some disease phenotypes, therefore it is very important for the cell nucleus imaging to diagnose some diseases, like trace nuclei, monitor the cell state and so on [17]. Mitochondria are the center of energy biogenesis because it can supply 90% of cellular energy via the electron transport chain. Mitochondrial dysfunction can trigger many aging and degenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, maternally inherited diabetes, cancer and so on [18]. Based on their importance, it is necessary to trace their locations and observe their morphological structures for the purposes of diagnosis and therapy.

In recent years, many increasing efforts have been made to improve the targeted properties of GQDs. There were a few reports on the application of GQDs for cell nucleus imaging. Also, Pan et al. fabricated GQDs by a hydrothermal cutting method and found that GQDs could accumulate around the cell nucleus [19]. Dong et al. synthesized GQDs from XC-72 carbon black by chemical oxidation and confirmed that GQDs could label the cell nucleus, but these GQDs had poor selectivity, in addition to the cell nucleus, some irrelative organelles could be labeled [20]. With respect to mitochondria imaging, Wang et al. developed a component by combing GQDs with aptamer AS1411 and demonstrated that it could accumulate in the mitochondria and lysosome, indicating that it had some potential for mitochondria imaging [21]. In addition to Wang's work, as far as we know, there were no other reports on GQDs used for mitochondria imaging. Currently, the biggest challenge for targeted imaging faced by GQDs is the lack of targeted selectivity. Most of the reported GQDs show poorly targeted selectivity, and some irrelative organelles may also be labeled besides the targeted organelles [15,22,23]. In addition, most of the reported GQDs with targeted properties which were prepared by the top-down method usually needed harsh reactive conditions and complex synthesis process, resulting in low production and limited application in the field of bioimaging [24–26]. Therefore, it is highly necessary to explore an efficient method to synthesize selectively targeted GQDs on a large scale.

Herein, we firstly reported a facile and large-scale preparation method to fabricate highly fluorescent water-soluble GQDs with targeted imaging property based on water-phase molecular fusion. The detailed synthesis process was illustrated in Fig. 1 , for the cell nucleus imaging, GQDs-polyethyleneimine (PEI) composite was prepared by using cheap and low-toxicity pyrene and PEI as precursors. The synthesis procedures involved 1,3,6-trinitropyrene preparation and hydrothermal reaction in an alkaline condition (PEI solution). For mitochondria imaging, GQDs-(3-carboxyl) phenyl bromide phosphine (TPP) composite was prepared by a simple amidation reaction between the carboxyl of TPP and amine of GQDs-PEI. The synthesized GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP showed low cytotoxicity and could be used for the cell nucleus imaging or mitochondria imaging, respectively. Meanwhile, both composites showed higher targeted selection than some reported GQDs.

PEI (Mn = 10,000), 3-(4,5-dimethyldiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), (3-carboxyl) phenyl bromide phosphine (TPP), 4′,6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI), Pyrene were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC. 1-Ethyl-3-(3dimethyllaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride was obtained from Shanghai Middle Qin Chemical Reagent co. LTD. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was acquired from Hangzhou Sijiqing Biological Engineering Materials Co., Ltd. Hela cell was obtained from the China Center for Type Culture Collection. All the other chemicals were analytical grade and used without purification. Ultrapure water (>18 MΩ cm) was used for rinsing and as the solvent as well.

1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene was synthesized according to Wang's method [27], and the obtained product was filtrated through a 0.45 μm microporous membrane to remove the acid, subsequently, the product was diluted to the concentration of 5 mg/mL with water. This solution was further used for the synthesis of GQDs-PEI. The detailed synthesis processes were shown as follows: 0.1 g PEI was added into the 40 mL 1,3,6-trinitropyrene solution, sealed in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove the large-size insoluble carbon particles, and then dialysed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 2 days. The purified solution was frozen, and then dried by sublimation in a vacuum chamber for 24 h. The dried sample was used for the following GQDs-TPP synthesis and structural characterization.

0.141 g TPP was dissolved in 100 mL 0.2 M PBS (pH 4.5) and stirred for 30 min under N2 protection. 0.124 g EDC was added and stirred for another 10 min. Subsequently, 50 mL dialysed GQDs-PEI was added into the mixed solution, and reacted for 2 h. After the reaction, the mixed solution was dialysed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) for 2 days. The purified solution was frozen, and then dried by sublimation in a vacuum chamber for 24 h.

The morphology, composition, and structure of the synthesized samples were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM − 2010), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/Max−2400 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation and graphite monochromator, λ = 1.54056 Å), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Bruker IFS66 V FTIR spectrometer), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI−5702, Physical Electronics, USA, using a monochromated Al Kα irradiation; the chamber pressure was ∼3 × 10−8 Torr under testing condition), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope III, Veeco). The absorption spectra of the GQDs were recorded using a UV/vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV/vis 2600). The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the synthesized samples were recorded with Fluorescence Spectrometer (Perkin, LS 55).
The quantum yield (Q) of the GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were determined by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the standard sample. Q can be calculated according to the following formula [27].

Qu = Qs×
Fu Fs
×
As
Au .
where F is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the optical density. The subscript “s” refers to standard with known quantum yield and “u” for the sample.

Hela cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics (60 μg/mL penicillin and 100 μg/mL streptomycin) at 37 °C with 5% CO2, awaiting the subsequent MTT assay. 200 μL cultured cells with a concentration of 1 × 106 cells/mL were added to 96 well plates and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. After that, 20 μL different concentrations of PEI, TPP, GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP (18.75, 37.5, 75, 150 and 300 μg/mL) were added into 96 well plates and cultured under the same conditions for another 24 h. Then 100 μL of MTT solution was injected into each well and the cells were cultured for another 4 h. The upper solvent was discarded, and then dissolving the remained blue formazan reaction product by addition of 200 μL of DMSO. The dissolvable solution was transferred to a 96-well plate, and the absorbance was recorded by using a microplate reader. Data were averaged from three parallel experiments.

Hela cells were seeded on glass coverslips in 6-well plates at 6000 cells per well in 2 mL of complete DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24 h. GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP with the concentration of 35 μg/mL were added to each well of 6-well plates, and cultured for 4 h. Subsequently, the cells were fixed in a 1 mL 4% paraformaldehyde solution for 30 min. The paraformaldehyde solution was discarded and the cells were rinsed with PBS three times. The cells were stained with 2 μL MitoTracker® Red for 30 min, and then they were further stained by the addition of 2 μL DAPI for 5 min. Fluorescent dye without entering cells was washed three times with PBS. The coverslips were mounted using one drop of Fluoromount™ Aqueous Mounting Medium and observed with an Olympus Fluoview 1000 confocal laser scanning microscope. The maximum excitation wavelengths of MitoTracker® Red, DAPI, and both GQDs composites were about 579 nm, 358 nm, and 488 nm, respectively.

The detailed synthesis process was illustrated in Fig. 1. Pyrene was nitrated into 1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene in a hot HNO3 solution, which had three positively charged sites of NO2 groups, enabling the nucleophilic substitution reactions to occur with many alkaline species such as OH−, NH3, and NH2 under the hydrothermal condition [28]. PEI is a polycation, alkaline polymer with a number of amine groups that can react with the NO2 groups of 1, 3, 6-trinitropyrene, facilitating the formation of chemical bonds between PEI and GQDs. For GQDs-TPP, the carboxyl of TPP can react with the amine groups of GQDs-PEI to form amido linkage. Therefore, GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP could be prepared in a facile and large-scale method (Fig. 1). According to the weight ratio of the product to the reagent, the yield of both composites could be calculated and found that the yield of two composites was higher than 60%. More importantly, we could get a gram scale when we used a 1 L poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. To confirm their chemical composition, FTIR was used and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 2 . For PEI, the absorption peaks located around 1568, 1660 and 1468 cm−1, corresponding to NH bending vibration of the primary amine, NH stretching vibration of the secondary amine and CN stretching vibration, respectively [29]. After being functionalized with PEI, the absorption peak of GQDs shifted from the wavelength of 1568 to 1507 cm−1 with a decreased peak intensity. It was different from the primary amine, the intensity of secondary amine located at 1660 cm−1 became stronger than before instead, indicating that nucleophilic substitution reaction took place between PEI and GQDs, and the PEI was grafted to the GQDs via the NH linkage. Meanwhile, CN stretching vibration could be observed at the absorption peak of 1385 cm−1 [30–32]. The results revealed that the GQDs was modified by the PEI and hydroxyl groups when it was synthesized in the presence of PEI. To synthesize the GQDs-TPP, we used carboxyl groups of TPP to react with the amine groups of PEI, following the formation of the amide bonds. As can be seen from Fig. 2, the characteristic carboxyl groups of TPP located at the wavelength of 1712 cm−1 [33,34] almost disappeared after they reacted with amine groups, meanwhile a new absorption peak appeared at 1656 cm−1 corresponding to amide I region [35]. What's more, the characteristic CBr stretching vibration of TPP at 530 cm−1 appeared in the spectrum of GQDs-TPP, indicating TPP was bond with the GQDs-PEI through amide bonds [36].

To analyze the composition of chemical bonds, XPS was used and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 3 . From the XPS survey spectra of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP (Fig. 3A), it could be found that GQDs-PEI was composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. After it reacted with TPP, a new element of phosphorus appeared in the spectrum. Fig. 3B and C were the high-resolution C1s spectrum of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP. As shown in Fig. 3B, the C1s spectra could be deconvoluted into three peaks, which centered at 284.7, 286.1 and 287.9 eV, corresponding to CC, CN, and CO, respectively [27,37,38]. After reaction with TPP, a new peak appeared at 288.2 eV in addition to CC and CN, which was assigned to CO (or CP), convincing that the amidation reaction occurred [39]. In addition, the N1 s spectrum of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were deconvoluted to differentiate its changes before and after the reaction. As to N1s of GQDs-PEI, it could be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 398.6, 399.3 and 400.7 eV, which can be ascribed to CN, NH2 and NH3 + (Fig. 3D), respectively [40–42]. After the reaction, a new peak appeared at 402 eV, corresponding to the NCO (Fig. 3E) [43]. The peak of P1s spectra of GQDs-TPP located at 131 eV (Fig. 3F), which could be attributed to CP, further proving that TPP was introduced into GQDs-PEI composite by amidation reaction [44].

The crystalline phases of the composites were investigated via XRD. As can be seen from Fig. 4A, the XRD pattern of PEI showed an amorphous peak around 2θ = 20.1°. After formation of GQDs-PEI, the characteristic (002) peak of GQDs appeared around 2θ = 25.8°, corresponding to an interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm [45]. The XRD pattern of GQDs-TPP showed the characteristic peaks of TPP except for the peak of GQDs, indicating that TPP was successfully integrated into the composite of GQDs-PEI. Meanwhile, we also used PEI as the precursor to conduct hydrothermal reaction, aiming to confirm the differences of the final product between pure PEI and the mixture of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and PEI, and the corresponding results, including XRD and FTIR were shown in Supporting Information (Fig. S1 and S2), which indicates that we can obtain the pure GQDs-PEI, rather than the mixture of GQDs-PEI and CQDs-PEI (Carbon Quantum Dots). The Raman spectra of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were shown in Fig. 4B, the GQDs-PEI showed broad peaks around 1378 and 1612 cm−1, corresponding to D and G bands [46]. It is well known the D band is associated with the sp3 carbon atoms of the defect structure, while the G band belongs to the first order scattering of the E2g phonon of the sp2 carbon atoms. The ratio between the D and G bands (ID/IG) can be used to estimate the degree of the structural disorder [47,48]. The D/G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) of GQDs-PEI was 0.9, indicating high crystal property of GQDs [27]. After being functionalized byTPP, D and G bands of GQDs shifted from 1378 and 1612 cm−1 to 1371 and 1600 cm−1, respectively. The redshift phenomenon may be caused by the π–π interactions between some TPP molecules and GQDs [49]. The D/G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) of GQDs-TPP was the same as the ratio of GQDs-PEI, implying that the TPP bond with the GQDs-PEI by amide linkages without causing more structural defects on the surface of GQDs.

In addition to chemical and structural composition, the morphology was characterized by TEM and AFM. As shown in Figs. 5A and B, TEM images of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP showed that two composites had a uniform size distribution ranging from 1.75 to 6.25 nm. The average size for GQDs-PEI was 3.75 nm. After the formation of GQDs-TPP, the average size was reduced to 3.25 nm, the reason may be that the transition from hydrophilicity to hydrophobicity caused the shrinkage of the molecular chain, leading to the reduction in the size. Meanwhile, the height profiles of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were observed by AFM (Figs. 5C and D). The height of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP was 3.5 nm and 4.6 nm respectively, which was higher than that of the reported GQDs without modification, but was similar to the GQDs modified by PEI [50], indicating the GQDs were modified by PEI or TPP.

The optical properties of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were shown in Fig. 6 . Both composites could emit blue fluorescence when they were irradiated by UV light at 365 nm, as shown in the insets of Figs. 6A and C. Most reported GQDs emitted green fluorescence, however, the GQDs after being functionalized by PEI and TPP emitted blue fluorescence. The PL shift maybe result from the charge transfers between GQDs and functional groups [51,52]. The quantum yields for GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were 12.9% and 7.3%, respectively, which was enough bright for bioimaging. The GQDs-PEI showed two absorption peaks located at 250 and 369 nm, respectively. Similarly, GQDs-TPP had two absorption peaks. One absorption peak kept at the same position with GQDs-PEI at 369 nm, while the other one was shifted to 230 nm. It is believed that the below 300 nm is assigned to π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, and 369 nm is ascribed to aromatic π orbitals [53,54]. The optimal maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs-PEI located at 369 and 475 nm, respectively. While the optimal maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs-TPP located at 369 and 450 nm. In addition, both composites showed excitation dependent PL behaviors, and the emission wavelengths redshifted when the excitation wavelengths changed from 350 nm to 440 nm. Different PL emission behavior of the GQDs is known to originate from different size, shape, and defects [55]. What's more, GQDs have more stable fluorescence than some fluorescent dyes, and they can still remain fluorescent stability when they are irradiated under UV light for several days, but the intensity of fluorescent dyes will decay and even disappear. Fluorescent stability of GQDs is one of the reasons why we fabricate them. We also detected the effect of different solvents on the fluorescent stability of the functionalized GQDs, including PBS (pH = 7.4), normal saline (pH = 7.0), fetal bovine serum and DMSO, which are often used in the cell culture. As shown in Fig. S3, we can see that the fluorescent intensity of the functionalized GQDs was almost stable in the solvents of PBS (pH = 7.4), normal saline (pH = 7.0), fetal bovine serum except DMSO. The reason for the decrease of fluorescent intensity in DMSO may be attributed to the poor solubility of the functionalized GQDs in the DMSO.

The excellent optical properties of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP can ensure the realization of cellular imaging. When biomaterials are used in vivo, it is significant to evaluate the biocompatibility. The biocompatibility of GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP were evaluated by MTT, a commonly used method to analyze the harmful effect of material to the cells, which based on the color change when yellow MTT salt is reductively cleaved to purple formazan by mitochondrial dehydrogenase of living cells. PEI, TPP, GQDs-PEI, and GQDs-TPP with different concentrations (18.75, 37.5, 150 and 300 μg/mL) were co-cultured with Hela cells for 24 h, and the results were shown in Fig. 7 . PEI had some toxicity to cells, and lower cell viability was observed. When it bonded with GQDs, the toxicity effect could be reduced, and higher cell viability could be observed, even if the concentration attained 300 μg/mL, the absorption value was still higher than 0.5. The reason may be that some amine groups of PEI can react with the NO2 groups of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, resulting in the reduction in the number of NH3 +, finally the alkalinity of GQDs-PEI is lower than the pure PEI, therefore the cellular viability is higher than PEI Compared to PEI, TPP had no toxicity to the cells, the cellular viability was almost similar to the control group. After it bonded with GQDs-PEI, the GQDs-TPP composite remained higher cellular viability than that of GQDs-PEI composite. The results showed that both composites had low cytotoxicity and could be used for cellular imaging.

The main purpose of this study is to determine if the synthesized GQDs can selectively target and image the cell nucleus and mitochondria. Two methods are commonly used to confirm the colocalization of two types of fluorescent dyes, including laser confocal fluorescence imaging and Image J software analysis. For laser confocal fluorescence imaging, it is very easy to see if two types of fluorescent dyes can overlap in the targeted organelles. As for Image J software analysis, we can also analyze if the peak positions of two types of fluorescent dyes can match, in addition, we can get the information of the fluorescent intensity [56]. To confirm if GQDs-PEI had an ability to target and image the cell nucleus, the commercial cell nucleus dye, DAPI with blue fluorescence was used as contrast agent because it can selectively stain the DNA of the cell nucleus. As shown in Fig. 8A, the cell nucleus can be stained by DAPI and showed a blue fluorescence, similarly, GQDs-PEI glowed strong green fluorescence and also appeared and lightened in the nucleus region (Fig. 8B). Meanwhile, we could see that the GQDs-PEI and DAPI had a good colocalization effect at the cell nucleus region (Fig. 8D). To further confirm the colocalization, software Image J analysis was used and the result was shown in Fig. 8E, we could see that the peak position and peak intensity of two types of fluorescent dyes could match very well, indicating that the synthesized GQDs-PEI had an excellent selectively targeting and imaging ability to the cell nucleus. More important, many studies have compared the fluorescent stability between the fluorescent dyes and GQDs, and found that the photostability of GQDs was superior to the fluorescent dyes [57], implying the better imaging effect can be obtained from GQDs-PEI. In addition, GQDs can be synthesized by using the cheap and low-toxicity pyrene and PEI as precursors, therefore the lower price of GQDs was another big advantage comparing to the fluorescent dyes. As cationic polymer, PEI was mainly used as the gene delivery, and many studies demonstrated that PEI could deliver the gene into the cell nucleus, but the mechanism remained unknown. The researchers speculated that PEI firstly entered the cytoplasm by the means of endocytosis, and then it could interact with anionic phospholipids that were continually synthesized for membrane regeneration. The lipid groups of phospholipids could interact with the nucleus membrane and fuse with the nucleus envelope, and ultimately entered the cell nucleus [58,59]. It is well known that the particles with the size below 9 nm could pass through the nuclear pore complexes [38]. In this study, the size of GQDs-PEI we prepared was smaller than 9 nm, enabling GQDs-PEI to freely diffuse into the cell nucleus. Some reported GQDs have some cell nucleus targeting abilities based on different precursors and synthesis methods. Such as nitrogen-doped carbon dots [60], cationic quaternized carbon dots (QCDs) and anionic graphene oxide sheets (GO) composite [61], zwitterionic carbon dots [39], and so on. Compared to above-mentioned materials, 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and PEI were chosen as the precursors, enabling to simplify the synthesis procedures and enhance the targeting selection to the cell nucleus. What's more, the obtained GQDs-PEI can be further functionalized through one-step and simple chemical modification for targeting mitochondria.

In addition to the cell nucleus imaging, the second purpose was to use GQDs-TPP for imaging mitochondria. It is well known that TPP is a cation with sufficient lipophilicity and delocalized positive charge, which can promote efficient accumulation in the mitochondria by reducing the free energy change on movement from an aqueous to a hydrophobic environment in response to the mitochondrial membrane potential [62]. Many studied have confirmed that conjugating TPP groups to various small molecules or even to a drug delivery system can obtain a good mitochondrial targeting effect [63,64]. In this study, in order to simplify the experimental process, we used TPP to modify the GQDs-PEI and synthesized a new GQDs-TPP composite with mitochondria targeting property. To embody the ability of mitochondria imaging of GQDs-TPP, MitoTracker® Red was used as the contrast agent because it can selectively stain the mitochondria in red fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 8H, mitochondria were stained by the MitoTracker® Red and showed red fluorescence. Compared with MitoTracker® Red, the mitochondria were stained with GQDs-TPP and exhibited green fluorescence. Meanwhile, these two fluorescences can overlap in the region of mitochondria (Fig. 8I). To further validate the colocalization effect, the software Image J was used and the result was shown in Fig. 8J, the peak position could match well, but the fluorescent intensity of GQDs-TPP was higher than that of MitoTracker®Red, indicating GQDs-TPP have good mitochondria targeting ability and can be used as a substitute for traditional fluorescent dyes for mitochondria imaging. Some research showed that the surface charge had an important impact on the mitochondria target [60], so we detected the zeta potential of the functionalized GQDs, and the results showed that the zeta potential of GQDs-PEI was 9.54 mV, while the zeta potential of GQDs-TPP was 15 mV. Therefore the zeta potential of GQDs-TPP was higher than that of GQDs-PEI and most reported GQDs [65–67], that may be another reason why GQDs-TPP can selectively target the mitochondria in addition to the effect of particle size.

In summary, in this study, we originally developed a facile, low-cost and large-scale method to synthesize GQDs, which can selectively target and image the cell nucleus and mitochondria, and the imaging properties of GQDs were comparable to the commercial fluorescent dyes, such as DAPI and MitoTracker® Red. Furthermore, the good biocompatibility, low-photobleaching, low-cost, and large-scale preparation made GQDs superior to commercial fluorescent dyes. Therefore, the GQDs are hoping to become the substitute of commercial fluorescent dyes, and will be widely applied in the fields of biomedicine. In addition to bioimaging, the synthesized GQDs can be used as the gene and drug delivery to fight some diseases associated with the cell nucleus and mitochondria.

In this study, GQDs with targeted properties for the cell nucleus or mitochondria imaging are prepared via a facile and large-scale preparation method, aiming at resolving the problems of the reported GQDs: (1) lack of targeted selection and specificity. (2) unachievable large-scale preparation via a facile and environmentally friendly method. (3) unreported mitochondria-targeted imaging. The prepared GQDs-PEI and GQDs-TPP can get a high yield over 60% with excellent optical property and low cytotoxicity. More importantly, both GQDs can selectively target and image the cell nucleus and mitochondria, respectively. The prepared GQDs can effectively overcome the drawbacks of cytotoxicity and photobleaching of traditional fluorescent dyes, which can be used as alternatives of traditional fluorescent dyes to locate and monitor the cell nucleus and mitochondria for biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We deeply appreciate the supports from Natural Science Foundation of China (81571829), the Medical Subject Fund of Stomatology College of Lanzhou University (201502-3), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2017-145, lzujbky-2017-it47), the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (LSL-1505), Cooperation Project between Gansu Academy of Sciences and Chinese Academy of Sciences (2016HZ-03), and Science and Technology Plan of Gansu Province (18JR3RA249).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.109824.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrolytic exfoliation synthesis of boron doped graphene quantum dots: a new luminescent material for electrochemiluminescence detection of oncogene microRNA-20a
